title: Double Wide Modern Chicken Coop
summary: A flat-roofed modern coop built as four pocket-holed wall panels — enclosed roost on one side, wire run on the other, with a metal roof sloped 5° across the whole footprint.
description:
Most coops look agricultural; this one looks like the shed a design blog would build — a low-slope (5°) metal roof over a clean T1-11 box, with the coop's two halves under one roofline: an enclosed roost chamber on one side, an open wire run on the other, split by an inner wall with its own T1-11 face.

The construction method is the modern part: PANELIZED. Each of the four walls builds flat as a complete 2x4 frame — pocket holes throughout, 1-1/2" setting, 2-1/2" exterior pocket screws — gets its siding or fencing while horizontal, and then the four finished panels screw together IN THE COOP'S FINAL LOCATION. Two people can raise it in an afternoon, and the source's staging tip (stain or paint panels BEFORE the fencing goes on) saves an hour of cutting-in around wire.

The 5° roof geometry lives in the side walls: their top rails are cut at 5° (91-1/2" long-point-to-short-point) and the stepped studs (53-1/4", 57-3/8", 61") carry the slope. The mirrored T1-11 side panels are cut 40-1/8" at the tall edge tapering to 35-7/8" — cut them in mirror or one panel's rough face ends up outside.

Doors — big front doors for run access, back doors for the roost — are measure-and-cut-to-fit, 1/4" smaller overall than their openings for 1/8" gaps, per the source's estimates-only framing. And the floor plywood is structural, not incidental: the source notes it squares and stiffens the coop, so screw it down properly.

Wire note as always: 'poultry fencing' keeps birds in; where raccoons roam, hardware cloth at reach height keeps everything else out.

tools.note: 1-1/2" setting, 2-1/2" exterior screws — the entire frame is pocket-holed panels.
The tapered T1-11 panels — cut the pair in mirror.
Poultry fencing to framing. Wire snips ride along.
The four panels get squared and plumbed at final assembly on real ground.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: All four wall panels, inner wall, doors, and roof framing.

materials.name: T1-11 siding, 1/2", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Side walls, inner wall, and door skins.

materials.name: CDX plywood, 1/2", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: The coop floor — it also squares the structure.

materials.name: Poultry fencing, 48" x 16 ft
materials.note: Run walls. Hardware cloth upgrade where predators are clever.

materials.name: Poultry fencing, 24" x 16 ft
materials.note: Back wall bottom and front door panels.

materials.name: Exterior paint or stain
materials.note: Applied to panels BEFORE fencing goes on.

cutList.note: Both ends 5° off square, ends parallel, long point to short point — the roof slope.
One end 5°, long point. The stepped studs carry the slope: 53-1/4", 57-3/8", 61".
Tapering to 35-7/8" at the short edge — CUT IN MIRROR.
ABOUT 68" — measure and cut to fit inside the enclosed area.
Set to match the side walls' roof plane.
Measure and cut to fit — it squares the coop.
Estimates — doors are built to openings minus 1/4" overall. Four at 28-5/8", four at 23-1/2".
Estimates — four at 60-3/4", four at 23-1/2". Cut to YOUR openings.

steps.title: Frame the mirrored side walls
steps.body:
Cut and lay out both side walls at once, in mirror, and confirm the mirror before drilling anything. Mark pocket hole locations, drill 1-1/2" pocket holes two per joint, and assemble with 2-1/2" exterior pocket screws.

The stepped studs and 5° top rail ARE the roof pitch — orient every 5° cut the same way within each wall, opposite between walls.

steps.title: Skin and wire the side panels
steps.body:
Cut the two tapered T1-11 panels in mirror and screw them to the INSIDE of each frame with 1-3/4" screws, finished face outward through the framing. Staple 48" poultry fencing over the open run areas and trim the excess.

Paint or stain each panel BEFORE its fencing goes on — the source's tip, and the difference between rolling a flat panel and detail-brushing around wire.

steps.title: Build the back and front walls
steps.body: Pocket-hole the back wall panel, finish it, and staple the 24" fencing across its lower section. Build the front wall panel the same way — its big openings become the wire doors later.

steps.title: Raise the box where it will live
steps.body: Carry the four panels to the coop's final spot and screw the corners together with 2-1/2" screws. Square the assembled frame by diagonals before the final screws go in — the floor panel will lock in whatever shape you leave here.

steps.title: Inner wall and interior framing
steps.body:
Build the inner wall frame (pocket holes and glue), face it with its T1-11 panel flush to side and top, and fit it inside the coop with 2-1/2" screws — it's the divider between roost and run.

Then measure and cut the interior 2x4s to fit: floor supports, and the roof support set to MATCH THE SIDE WALLS' ROOF PLANE — the roofing lands on all of it at once, so a proud support here is a dent in the metal later.

steps.title: Floor in
steps.body: Measure, cut, and screw down the 1/2" CDX floor in the roost chamber. The source is explicit that the floor helps the structure — it's the diaphragm that keeps the box square, so screw it to every support it crosses.

steps.title: Doors — cut to YOUR openings
steps.body:
Measure each opening and build each 2x4 door frame 1/4" smaller overall (1/8" gap per side). Back doors get T1-11 skins — account for the floor's thickness when placing the skin, per the source. Front doors get poultry fencing stapled to their frames.

Hang everything on the exterior hinges with pulls and two-motion latches. Ten hinges sounds like a lot until you've met a coop with too few doors; you never clean through a hatch you wish were bigger.

steps.title: Roof on
steps.body:
Center the two metal panels for even overhang on all four sides and drive the gasketed roofing screws into the four walls and the roof framing — through the flats, snug, not crushed.

The 5° slope is subtle by design; check that it drains AWAY from the run door before you commit the screws. Then introduce the hens to modern architecture.

images.alt: Modern flat-roofed double-wide chicken coop with enclosed roost and wire run
